policy_statement: Label 100% of the city's green spaces as “Réseau Nature” by 2030 under differentiated management criteria including absence of synthetic pesticides and prioritising indigenous plants.
mechanism_description: The city will develop a differentiated green space management system so green spaces meet “Réseau Nature” criteria (including no synthetic pesticides and prioritizing indigenous plants), improving urban ecosystem condition and resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem enhancement → ↑ biodiversity/ecosystem function → ↓ heat exposure + ↑ carbon sinks
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) System changed: municipal green space operations (plant selection, mowing/pruning regimes, soil and pest management) shift to pesticide-free, native-species-oriented, biodiversity-supporting “differentiated management.” (2) Mediating process: healthier soils and vegetation structure increase habitat quality, ecological connectivity, evapotranspiration and shading, and can increase biomass/soil organic carbon while reducing ecological stressors from agrochemicals. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: reduced urban heat island impacts and improved resilience to heat/drought/extremes, with ancillary local carbon sequestration benefits.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved ecosystem function in urban green spaces that reduces heat exposure (urban cooling) and can marginally increase local carbon sequestration.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: management changes enhance ecosystems, yielding cooling/resilience benefits (and some sequestration), i.e., “ecosystem enhancement → climate benefit.”  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: it is not primarily about reducing energy/material inputs (Resource Efficiency) nor directly cutting GHG emissions through technology/fuel switching (Mitigation); the climate benefit arises through ecological function, matching Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: while pesticide bans and operational changes could reduce minor upstream emissions, that is not the dominant climate effect; similarly, adaptation-like outcomes (heat risk reduction) are delivered via ecosystem enhancement, which is still categorized as NBS under the given taxonomy.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: any policy that implements biodiversity-oriented ecological management of urban green spaces has its main climate relevance through improved ecosystem condition (cooling/resilience and limited sequestration), so NBS is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., primarily cooling/adaptation vs carbon sink) is deferred to policy-level evidence on design, scale, and monitored outcomes.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a specific program is framed mainly as heatwave risk reduction (e.g., targeted shade provision) it could resemble Adaptation, but under this canonical mechanism the intervention is explicitly ecosystem/biodiversity restoration via green-space ecological management, so Nature-Based Solutions remains primary.
key_indicators: “100% of the city's green spaces to be labelled Réseau Nature by 2030”; “differentiated management system for green spaces”; “meeting the criteria of the ‘Réseau Nature’ label”; “absence of synthetic pesticides”; “prioritising indigenous plants”
co_benefits: Improved public health (reduced pesticide exposure); enhanced urban amenity/recreation; pollinator support and broader biodiversity gains.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a performance-standard/regulatory approach if the “Réseau Nature” criteria are mandatory requirements for municipal park management; however, the phrasing emphasizes a percentage-by-date objective (target) and labeling rather than an enforceable standard with compliance provisions.
